sources.exact_document_title: Shoulder Pain: Diagnosis, Treatment and Referral Guidelines for Primary, Community and Intermediate Care
sources.version: BESS Patient Care Pathways summary; Shoulder & Elbow 13(1):5–11; DOI 10.1177/1758573220984471
sources.population: Adults with common shoulder conditions in primary, community, or intermediate care.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, and intermediate musculoskeletal care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for shoulder red flags, broad assessment, subacromial pain, frozen shoulder, glenohumeral arthritis, and instability; international and partial for a generic UAE shoulder-pain workflow.

exact_sections.heading: Shared Decision Making and Urgent Red Flag Referrals for the Shoulder
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires direct clinician-patient decisions and lists infection, unreduced dislocation, tumour, traumatic acute cuff tear, and inflammatory disease as urgent concern contexts.

exact_sections.heading: Subacromial Shoulder Pain — definition and diagnosis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports rotator-cuff or impingement terminology and history covering hand dominance, occupation, activity, pain location, radiation, onset, duration, aggravating factors, trauma, comorbidities, red flags, and passive external rotation.

exact_sections.heading: Subacromial Shoulder Pain — imaging and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports selected two-view radiography, states ultrasound or MRI is rarely indicated without an agreed specialist pathway, and links persistent symptoms to clinician-arranged referral.

exact_sections.heading: Frozen shoulder, glenohumeral arthritis, and instability summaries
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports range-of-motion, stiffness, function, instability, trauma, recurrent dislocation, and clinician-selected imaging or referral documentation across common shoulder presentations.

sources.issuing_organisation: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists
sources.exact_document_title: The Initial Management of Chronic Pelvic Pain — Green-top Guideline No. 41
sources.version: Second edition; Green-top Guideline No. 41; third edition in development
sources.population: Women presenting for the first time with chronic pelvic pain of at least 6 months that is not pregnancy-related.
sources.clinical_setting: General gynaecology and primary-care referral assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for the chronic non-pregnancy subset only; old, international, and partial for acute, pregnancy-related, infectious, or emergency pelvic-pain presentations.

exact_sections.heading: 1. Purpose and Scope; 2. Background; 5. Initial Assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines chronic pelvic pain as a symptom rather than a diagnosis and frames initial assessment around the woman’s concerns, function, and multifactorial context.

exact_sections.heading: 5.1 History; 5.2 Examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies pain pattern, menstrual, bladder, bowel, movement, posture, function and medication history plus abdominal and pelvic examination for focal tenderness or structural findings.

exact_sections.heading: 6.1 Screening for Infection; 6.2 Transvaginal Scanning and MRI
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-selected STI sampling when PID is suspected and transvaginal ultrasound for adnexal masses and selected chronic pelvic-pain pathology.
